dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3, responding for chest pain at 1234 Main St. Female, 49 years old, mild respiratory distress. Arrival expected in 5 minutes.
Hi ma'am, I'm Jordan, an EMT with the rescue squad. What led you to call 911? We've already donned our PPE.
I woke up with shortness of breath and chest pain. There's also a red, warm lump on my right ankle. It's been going on for over an hour.
Just to check in, what's your full name again?
My name is Susan Smith. I was born on August 14, 1973.
Can you tell me the name of the county we're in? And what day is it today?
We are in Chesterfield County, and today is August 5th, 2020.
Great, Susan. Can you lift your right hand for me?
(Lifts her right hand.) It's here.
Are you alert to your surroundings? Can you tell me the current month?
I'm alert. It's August now.
And do you know where you are? What year do we live in?
I'm at home. It's the year 2020.
Thank you, Susan. Do you remember who you are? What your job is?
I remember I'm Susan Smith. I'm a freelance artist.
To understand your breathing better, I'll listen to your lungs. This will be quick and not uncomfortable.

Respiratory rate is 30, regular rise and fall, chest symmetrically moves. No cyanosis noted. SpO₂ is 99%. Breath sounds are clear bilaterally. Radial pulse is strong and present. Skin is pink, warm, and dry.
Okay, we're clear. Let's go over a few more details while I send another assessment.

Blood pressure is 168/88 with a pulse of 100 and regular. Glucose is 129. First IV attempt unsuccessful. Skin temp checked; skin is not warm or cold. Skin is well perfused with no signs of a shock state.
Susan, when did the shortness of breath and chest pain begin exactly?
It started in the middle of the night. I took my albuterol, but it didn't help. I also noticed a painful red lump on my right ankle today.
Does the chest pain come with breathing or touch? Have you had other symptoms like fever or dizziness?

The chest pain comes with breathing and touch on the ankle. No fever or dizziness. I missed dialysis since Saturday because I had a fever at that time. I've had a previous pulmonary embolism but no anticoagulants now. No fall or injury since.
Can you rate the chest and ankle pain on a scale from 0 to 10?
My chest is a 10, and the ankle is an 8. The chest is sharp and piercing, gets worse when I breathe deeply, and it hasn't spread. The ankle pain is similar but constant.
Understood. Has the pain changed over time in character or location?
No, it's all central to the chest and ankle with no movement.
I see the red lump on your ankle; it's warm and tender. Do you see any open areas or signs of injury on it?
There's no wound, just the lump. Can you check the size?
Reassess your lungs. Take a deep breath in slowly and then let it out.
Breath sounds still equal and clear bilaterally. No wheezes or crackles. Time to get a 12-lead ECG. SpO₂ stays at 99%. Pulse ox remains stable.
You've mentioned being on dialysis. Have your urination or back pain changed anything?
I missed last Saturday's session due to fever, so my legs feel a little puffy. My back pain is still the same.
Let me check that right ankle bump. Tenderness right there?
Yes, there's some tenderness at the lower part. Left side of my ankle is completely normal.
Pulse is now 87, stable and regular. We'll keep an eye on it.
Respiratory rate has improved slightly to 26. Still feeling a bit short on air, but more controlled now.
Blood pressure is 156/74 and the pulse remains 87. That is lower than before but still stable.
SpO₂ is up to 94%. EKG shows sinus rhythm with occasional PVCs. Signs look the same.
Blood glucose is zero from the last strip. Needs to be rechecked with a new strip again.
Rechecking the 12-lead ECG again. Still shows no ST elevation or other acute findings. Keep monitoring vitals and EKG changes at regular intervals.
It's time for 324 mg of ASA PO. It helps with chest pain and possible ACS. Okay if we give it now?
Go ahead. I don't have any problems with aspirin.
We've reviewed your history, and we cross-checked with Jordan. Giving you the ASA now with some water.
The ASA has been given with some water. It will start to take effect. Keep breathing regularly. SpO₂ is now 94% and EKG stable.
Now placing you on the cardiac monitor with a 4-lead ECG in addition to the 12-lead we already obtained. Keep monitoring for irregularities.
Triggering Protocol 3-11 for chest pain and possible respiratory distress. It also covers your missing dialysis and leg findings.
One more vitals check at 23:50:27. SpO₂ is 96%, pulse remains 87, respiratory rate is 30, blood pressure 167/104. Blood glucose is still zero.
Patient is tolerating ASA well. IV start still unsuccessful. EKG persisting in normal sinus rhythm with occasional PVCs.
Let's check in again. Still 10/10 chest pain and 8/10 on the ankle? No increase or spread?
Yes, still the same and localized.
Anything else we should know about your condition before we move forward?
You have covered the important parts - the chest pain, past PE, missed dialysis, and the AMA.
We are transporting directly to VCU. It's the best for your condition with your dialysis history. We'll be careful with handling.
That's okay. Please move me carefully.
We'll help you to the ambulance now. Take your own time moving. We'll assist you to the stretcher and set up for transport.
Ready for hospital handoff. ASA administered, EKG stable. EKG shows sinus rhythm with PVC. Afebrile and alert to person, place, and time.
History of PE, missed dialysis. IV not successful at this time. EKG, vitals, and ASA on board. Consent documents are signed.
